t: A pizza has 8 equal slices. Jade eats 3/8 and her mom eats 2/8. What fraction is left? Is more or less than half left?
e: 3/8 + 2/8 = 5/8 eaten. 8/8 - 5/8 = 3/8 left. Half is 4/8, and 3/8 < 4/8, so less than half is left.

t: Ravi says 1/8 is bigger than 1/2 because 8 is bigger than 2. What is wrong with Ravi's thinking?
o: More pieces means each piece is smaller, so 1/8 < 1/2
You cannot compare fractions with different bottoms
e: Ravi is wrong. When you cut into MORE pieces, each piece is SMALLER. 1/8 is much smaller than 1/2.

t: You have 9 eighths of a cake. Is that more or less than 1 whole cake? By how much?
e: 8/8 = 1 whole. 9/8 = 8/8 + 1/8. So you have 1 whole cake plus 1/8 more.

t: 2 kids share 1 pie equally. Then they get a second pie and share it equally too. What fraction of a pie does each kid have now?
e: Each kid gets 1/2 from the first pie and 1/2 from the second pie. 1/2 + 1/2 = 2/2 = 1 whole pie each.

t: A rectangle is split into 8 parts. 3 parts are tall and 5 are short. Can you say 5/8 is shaded if the short parts are colored?
o: Yes, because 5 of 8 parts are colored
No, because the parts are not all equal
Yes, because most of the parts are colored
No, because you can only shade half
e: The parts are not equal (3 tall, 5 short). Fractions require all parts to be the same size, so 5/8 is not correct.

t: Noah shades 2/4 of a square. Eva shades 4/8 of the same size square. Finn shades 1/2 of the same size square. Who shaded the most?
o: They all shaded the same amount
e: 2/4 = 1/2 and 4/8 = 1/2. All three fractions are equal -- they all shaded exactly half.

t: 4 friends share 3 pies equally. What fraction of a pie does each friend get?
e: 3 pies split among 4 friends = 3/4 per friend. Each person gets 3 fourths of a pie.

t: Amara ate 1/4 of a cake on Saturday, 1/4 on Sunday, and 1/4 on Monday. How much has she eaten? How much is left?
e: 1/4 + 1/4 + 1/4 = 3/4 eaten. The whole cake is 4/4, so 4/4 - 3/4 = 1/4 is left.

t: Leo says 2/2 is bigger than 4/4 because halves are bigger than fourths. Is Leo right?
e: Leo is wrong. 2/2 = 1 whole and 4/4 = 1 whole. They are the same amount!

t: A cookie sheet has 8 equal cookies. Mom takes 1/2 of them for a party and Dad takes 1/4 of the total. How many cookies are left?
e: Mom takes 1/2 of 8 = 4 cookies. Dad takes 1/4 of 8 = 2 cookies. 4 + 2 = 6 taken. 8 - 6 = 2 cookies left.

t: Which list shows fractions from smallest to largest?
e: 1/8 is the smallest piece, then 1/4, then 1/2 is the biggest. More pieces = smaller slices.

t: Kai has 3 fourths of a granola bar. Zoe has 6 eighths of the same size bar. Who has more?
e: 3/4 = 6/8 because each fourth splits into 2 eighths. 3 x 2 = 6 eighths. They are equal!

t: Ava has 5 halves of a sandwich. How many whole sandwiches can she make? How much is left over?
e: 2 halves = 1 whole. 4 halves = 2 wholes. The 5th half is left over. So 2 wholes and 1/2.

t: A pan of brownies has 8 equal pieces. 3 kids each take 2 pieces. Is there enough for a 4th kid to also take 2 pieces?
e: 3 kids x 2 pieces = 6 pieces taken. 8 - 6 = 2 pieces left. Yes, 1 more kid can take 2 pieces!

t: Ian says if two fractions have the same top number, the one with the bigger bottom number is bigger. Is Ian correct?
o: Yes, bigger bottom means bigger fraction
No, bigger bottom means smaller pieces so the fraction is smaller
Yes, because more parts is always more
e: Ian is wrong. With the same top number, a bigger bottom means more pieces, so each piece is smaller. Example: 1/8 < 1/4.

t: Mia colors 1/2 of a shape red, then 1/4 of the same shape blue. What fraction of the shape is still white?
e: 1/2 = 2/4. Red is 2/4 and blue is 1/4. That is 2/4 + 1/4 = 3/4 colored. So 4/4 - 3/4 = 1/4 is white.

t: A ribbon is 1 whole long. You cut off 3/8. Then you cut off another 3/8. How much ribbon is left?
Ella says 4/4, 2/2, and 8/8 are all different amounts. Is she right?
o: No, only 4/4 and 2/2 are the same
e: Ella is wrong. 4/4 = 1 whole, 2/2 = 1 whole, and 8/8 = 1 whole. When top equals bottom, it is always 1 whole.

t: 2 friends want to share 3 granola bars equally. What fraction does each friend get?
e: 3 bars shared by 2 friends = 3/2 per friend. That is 1 whole bar and 1/2 more (one and a half bars).

t: Jada says cutting a pizza into 8 slices gives you more pizza than cutting it into 4 slices. What is wrong with her thinking?
o: The total pizza stays the same; more slices just means smaller slices
You actually get less pizza with more cuts
Pizzas can only be cut into 4 slices
e: Jada is wrong. Cutting more slices does not create more pizza. The total stays the same -- each slice just gets smaller.
